question: Does the paper report HIV sequences from patient samples?
evidence: Here, we report application of high-throughput near-full-length genome (NFLG) and partial HIV-1 proviral genome deep sequencing to characterize HIV in recently infected blood donors at four major blood centers in Brazil. Of the 47 recently infected donor samples studied, 39 (82.9%) NFLGs and 6 (12.7%) partial fragments were *de novo* assembled into contiguous sequences and successfully subtyped.
rationale: The evidence clearly states that the study involved the sequencing of HIV from patient samples, specifically from recently infected blood donors in Brazil.

evidence: Here, we report application of high-throughput near-full-length genome (NFLG) and partial HIV-1 proviral genome deep sequencing to characterize HIV in recently infected blood donors at four major blood centers in Brazil. From 2007-2011, 341 HIV+ blood donors from 4 blood centers were recruited to participate in a case control study to identify HIV risk factors and motivations to donate. Forty-seven (17 from São Paulo [SP], 8 from Minas Gerais [MG], 11 from Pernambuco [PE] and 11 from Rio de Janeiro [RJ]) were classified as recently infected based on testing by less-sensitive enzyme immunoassays.
